[Cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the Winter Olympics"), [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_Olympic_Games "Figure skating at the Olympic Games"), [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games"), [Nordic combined](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Nordic combined at the Winter Olympics"), [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Ski jumping at the Winter Olympics"), and [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the Winter Olympics") have been featured at every Winter Olympics programme since its inception in [1924](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics"). Current Olympic sports, like [badminton](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Badminton at the Summer Olympics"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the Summer Olympics"), and [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Volleyball at the Summer Olympics"), first appeared on the programme as [demonstration sports](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") and were later promoted to full Olympic sports. Some sports that were featured in earlier Games were later dropped from the programme.[[173]](#cite_note-176)

Olympic sports are governed by [international sports federations](/wiki/List_of_international_sports_federations "List of international sports federations") (IFs) recognised by the IOC as the global supervisors of those sports. There are 35 federations represented at the IOC.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlympic_Charter200788–90-177) There are sports recognised by the IOC that are not included in the Olympic programme. These sports are not considered Olympic sports, but they can be promoted to this status during a programme revision that occurs in the first IOC session following a celebration of the Olympic Games.[[175]](#cite_note-178)[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlympic_Charter200787-179) During such revisions, sports can be excluded or included in the programme on the basis of a two-thirds majority vote of the members of the IOC.[[177]](#cite_note-180) There are recognised sports that have never been on an Olympic programme in any capacity, for example, orienteering.[[178]](#cite_note-recognizedsports-181)

In October and November 2004, the IOC established an Olympic Programme Commission, tasked with reviewing the sports on the Olympic programme and all non-Olympic recognised sports. The goal was to apply a systematic approach to establishing the Olympic programme for each celebration of the Games.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) The commission formulated seven criteria to judge whether a sport should be included on the Olympic programme.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) These criteria are history and tradition of the sport, universality, popularity of the sport, image, athletes' health, development of the International Federation that governs the sport, and costs of holding the sport.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) From this study five recognised sports emerged as candidates for inclusion at the 2012 Summer Olympics: golf, karate, rugby sevens, roller sports and [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)").[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) These sports were reviewed by the IOC Executive Board and then referred to the General Session in Singapore in July 2005. Of the five sports recommended for inclusion only two were selected as finalists: karate and squash.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) Neither sport attained the required two-thirds vote and consequently they were not promoted to the Olympic programme.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) In October 2009 the IOC voted to instate golf and rugby sevens as Olympic sports for the [2016](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") and [2020 Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympic_Games "2020 Summer Olympic Games").[[180]](#cite_note-newosports-183)

The 114th [IOC Session](/wiki/IOC_Session "IOC Session"), in 2002, limited the Summer Games programme to a maximum of 28 sports, 301 events, and 10,500 athletes.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) Three years later, at the [117th IOC Session](/wiki/117th_IOC_Session "117th IOC Session"), the first major programme revision was performed, resulting in the exclusion of [baseball](/wiki/Baseball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Baseball at the Summer Olympics") and [softball](/wiki/Softball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Softball at the Summer Olympics") from the official programme of the [2012 London Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"). Since there was no agreement in the promotion of two other sports, the 2012 programme featured just 26 sports.[[179]](#cite_note-factsheetsports-182) The 2016 and 2020 Games returned to the maximum of 28 sports given the addition of rugby and golf.[[180]](#cite_note-newosports-183)

### Amateurism and professionalism

Further information: [Amateur sports](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg/250px-Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg)

The Gold medal game between [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team") and [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") at the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics"); professional [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") players have been eligible to participate in [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games") since then.

The [ethos](/wiki/Ethos "Ethos") of the aristocracy as exemplified in the [English public school](/wiki/English_public_school "English public school") greatly influenced [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin").[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEassom1994120–123-184) The public schools subscribed to the belief that sport formed an important part of education, an attitude summed up in the saying *[mens sana in corpore sano](/wiki/Mens_sana_in_corpore_sano "Mens sana in corpore sano")*, a healthy mind in a healthy body. In this ethos, a gentleman was one who became an all-rounder, not the best at one specific thing. There was also a prevailing concept of fairness, in which practising or training was considered tantamount to cheating.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEassom1994120–123-184) Those who practised a sport professionally were considered to have an unfair advantage over those who practised it merely as a hobby.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEassom1994120–123-184)

The exclusion of professionals caused several controversies throughout the history of the modern Olympics. The [1912 Olympic](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") pentathlon and [decathlon](/wiki/Decathlon "Decathlon") champion [Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe") was stripped of his medals when it was discovered that he had taken expense money playing semi-professional baseball before the Olympics. His medals were posthumously restored by the IOC in 1983 after a ruling found that the decision to strip him of his medals fell [outside of the required 30-day period](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1912_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_decathlon "Athletics at the 1912 Summer Olympics – Men's decathlon").[[182]](#cite_note-185) Swiss and Austrian skiers boycotted the [1936 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1936_Winter_Olympics "1936 Winter Olympics") in support of their skiing teachers, who were not allowed to compete because they earned money with their sport and were thus considered professionals.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

The advent of the state-sponsored "full-time amateur athlete" of the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") countries eroded the ideology of the pure [amateur](/wiki/Amateur_sports#Olympics "Amateur sports"), as it put the self-financed amateurs of the Western countries at a disadvantage. The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_Olympics "Soviet Union at the Olympics") entered teams of athletes who were all nominally students, soldiers, or working in a profession, but in reality they were all paid by the state to train on a full-time basis.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEassom1994120–123-184)[[184]](#cite_note-187)[[185]](#cite_note-cia.gov-188) The situation greatly disadvantaged American and Western European athletes, and this was a significant factor in the decline of American medal hauls in the 1970s and 1980s.[[186]](#cite_note-189) As a result, the Olympics shifted away from amateurism, as envisioned by Pierre de Coubertin, to allowing participation of [professional athletes](/wiki/Professional_athletes "Professional athletes"),[[187]](#cite_note-Amateurism-190) but only in the 1990s, after the [collapse of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_Soviet_Union "Collapse of the Soviet Union") and its influence within the [IOC](/wiki/IOC "IOC").[[188]](#cite_note-191)[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193)

#### Team Canada ice hockey dispute

Near the end of the 1960s, the [Canadian Amateur Hockey Association](/wiki/Canadian_Amateur_Hockey_Association "Canadian Amateur Hockey Association") (CAHA) felt their amateur players could no longer be competitive against the Soviet team's full-time athletes and the other constantly improving European teams. They pushed for the ability to use players from professional leagues but met opposition from the [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF) and IOC. At the IIHF Congress in 1969, the IIHF decided to allow Canada to use nine non-NHL professional hockey players[[191]](#cite_note-Num17-194) at the 1970 World Championships in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), Canada.[[192]](#cite_note-195) The decision was reversed in January 1970 after Brundage said that ice hockey's status as an Olympic sport would be in jeopardy if the change was made.[[191]](#cite_note-Num17-194) In response, Canada withdrew from international ice hockey competition and officials stated that they would not return until "[open competition](/wiki/Open_(sport) "Open (sport)")" was instituted.[[191]](#cite_note-Num17-194)[[193]](#cite_note-196) [Günther Sabetzki](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Sabetzki "Günther Sabetzki") became president of the IIHF in 1975 and helped to resolve the dispute with the CAHA. In 1976, the IIHF agreed to allow "open competition" between all players in the World Championships. However, NHL players were still not allowed to play in the Olympics until 1988, because of the IOC's amateur-only policy.[[194]](#cite_note-197)

## Controversies

Main article: [List of Olympic Games scandals and controversies](/wiki/List_of_Olympic_Games_scandals_and_controversies "List of Olympic Games scandals and controversies")

### Boycotts

Main article: [List of Olympic Games boycotts](/wiki/List_of_Olympic_Games_boycotts "List of Olympic Games boycotts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/1956_Summer_Olympics_%28Melbourne%29_boycotting_countries_%28blue%29.png/250px-1956_Summer_Olympics_%28Melbourne%29_boycotting_countries_%28blue%29.png)](/wiki/File:1956_Summer_Olympics_(Melbourne)_boycotting_countries_(blue).png)

Countries that boycotted the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") (shaded blue)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/1980_Summer_Olympics_%28Moscow%29_boycotting_countries_%28blue%29.svg/250px-1980_Summer_Olympics_%28Moscow%29_boycotting_countries_%28blue%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1980_Summer_Olympics_(Moscow)_boycotting_countries_(blue).svg)

[Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") are the only countries to be represented at every Olympic Games since their inception in 1896. While countries sometimes miss an Olympics due to a lack of qualified athletes, some choose to boycott a celebration of the Games for various reasons. The [Olympic Council of Ireland](/wiki/Olympic_Council_of_Ireland "Olympic Council of Ireland") withdrew from the [1936 Berlin Games](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics"), because the IOC insisted its team needed to be restricted to the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") rather than representing the entire island of Ireland.[[195]](#cite_note-Krüger_Murray_2003_p230-198)

There were three boycotts of the [1956 Melbourne Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics"): the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") refused to attend because of the repression of the [Hungarian Revolution](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956") by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") but did send an equestrian delegation to Stockholm; [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), and [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") boycotted the Games because of the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis"); and the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") boycotted the Games due to the participation of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China"), composed of athletes coming from [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[196]](#cite_note-Boycott_1-199)

In [1972](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") and [1976](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") a large number of African countries threatened the IOC with a boycott to force them to ban [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Rhodesia](/wiki/Rhodesia "Rhodesia"), because of their [segregationist](/wiki/Segregationist "Segregationist") rule. [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") was also one of the African boycott targets, because [its national rugby union team](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") had toured [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid")-ruled South Africa. The IOC conceded in the first two cases but refused to ban New Zealand on the grounds that rugby was not an Olympic sport.[[197]](#cite_note-CBC_boycott-200) Fulfilling their threat, twenty African countries were joined by [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana") and Iraq in a withdrawal from the Montreal Games, after a few of their athletes had already competed.[[197]](#cite_note-CBC_boycott-200)[[198]](#cite_note-201)

The Republic of China (Taiwan) was excluded from the [1976 Games](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") by order of [Pierre Trudeau](/wiki/Pierre_Trudeau "Pierre Trudeau"), the prime minister of Canada. Trudeau's action was widely condemned as having brought shame on Canada for having succumbed to political pressure to keep the Chinese delegation from competing under its name.[[199]](#cite_note-202) The ROC refused a proposed compromise that would have still allowed them to use the [ROC flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Taiwan "Flag of Taiwan") and [anthem](/wiki/National_Anthem_of_the_Republic_of_China "National Anthem of the Republic of China") as long as the name was changed.[[200]](#cite_note-203) Athletes from Taiwan did not participate again until the [1984 Los Angeles Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics"), when they returned under the name of Chinese Taipei and with a special flag and anthem.[[201]](#cite_note-corbit-204)

In 1980 and 1984, the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") opponents boycotted each other's Games. The [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and sixty-five other countries boycotted the [Moscow Olympics in 1980](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics") because of the [Soviet invasion of Afghanistan](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War"). This boycott reduced the number of nations participating to 80, the lowest number since 1956.[[202]](#cite_note-205) The Soviet Union and 15 other nations countered by boycotting the Los Angeles Olympics of 1984. Although a boycott led by the Soviet Union depleted the field in certain sports, 140 National Olympic Committees took part, which was a record at the time.[[5]](#cite_note-olympic.org-6) The fact that Romania, a Warsaw Pact country, opted to compete despite Soviet demands led to a warm reception of the Romanian team by the United States. When the Romanian athletes entered during the opening ceremonies, they received a standing ovation from the spectators, who comprised mostly U.S. citizens. The boycotting nations of the Eastern Bloc staged their own alternate event, the [Friendship Games](/wiki/Friendship_Games "Friendship Games"), in July and August.[[203]](#cite_note-1980_Boycott-206)[[204]](#cite_note-1980_Boycott_2-207)

There had been growing calls for boycotts of Chinese goods and the 2008 Olympics in Beijing in protest of China's [human rights record](/wiki/Human_rights_in_China "Human rights in China"), and in response to [Tibetan disturbances](/wiki/2008_Tibetan_unrest "2008 Tibetan unrest"). Ultimately, no nation supported a boycott.[[205]](#cite_note-208)[[206]](#cite_note-EUTibet-209) In August 2008, the government of [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") called for a boycott of the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics"), set to be held in [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), Russia, in response to Russia's participation in the [2008 South Ossetia war](/wiki/2008_South_Ossetia_war "2008 South Ossetia war").[[207]](#cite_note-210)[[208]](#cite_note-211) Continuing human rights violations in China have led to "[diplomatic boycotts](/wiki/Concerns_and_controversies_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics#Diplomatic_boycotts_or_non-attendance "Concerns and controversies at the 2022 Winter Olympics")", where athletes still compete at the Games but diplomats do not attend, of the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics") in Beijing by several countries, most notably the United States and United Kingdom.[[209]](#cite_note-212)[[210]](#cite_note-213)

### Politics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-G00630%2C_Sommerolympiade%2C_Siegerehrung_Weitsprung.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-G00630%2C_Sommerolympiade%2C_Siegerehrung_Weitsprung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-G00630,_Sommerolympiade,_Siegerehrung_Weitsprung.jpg)

[Jesse Owens](/wiki/Jesse_Owens "Jesse Owens") on the podium after winning the long jump at the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics"), where he won four gold medals; in his 1980 obituary, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* called him "perhaps the greatest and most famous athlete in [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") history".[[211]](#cite_note-214)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/John_Carlos%2C_Tommie_Smith%2C_Peter_Norman_1968cr.jpg/250px-John_Carlos%2C_Tommie_Smith%2C_Peter_Norman_1968cr.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Carlos,_Tommie_Smith,_Peter_Norman_1968cr.jpg)

Gold medallist [Tommie Smith](/wiki/Tommie_Smith "Tommie Smith") (centre) and bronze medallist [John Carlos](/wiki/John_Carlos "John Carlos") (right) showing the [raised fist](/wiki/Raised_fist "Raised fist") on the podium after the 200 m race at the [1968 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics"); both are wearing [Olympic Project for Human Rights](/wiki/Olympic_Project_for_Human_Rights "Olympic Project for Human Rights") badges. [Peter Norman](/wiki/Peter_Norman "Peter Norman") (silver medallist, left) from Australia is also wearing an OPHR badge, in solidarity with Smith and Carlos.

The Olympic Games have been used as a platform to promote political ideologies almost from its inception. Nazi Germany wished to portray the [National Socialist Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") as benevolent and peace-loving when they hosted the [1936 Games](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics"), though they used the Games to display [Aryan](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan") superiority.[[30]](#cite_note-FindlingPelle-32): 107 Germany was the most successful nation at the Games, adding support to their allegations of Aryan supremacy, but the message was somewhat blunted by the notable victories of African American [Jesse Owens](/wiki/Jesse_Owens "Jesse Owens"), who won four gold medals, and [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungary_at_the_1936_Summer_Olympics "Hungary at the 1936 Summer Olympics") Jew [Ibolya Csák](/wiki/Ibolya_Cs%C3%A1k "Ibolya Csák").[[30]](#cite_note-FindlingPelle-32): 111–112 The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_Olympics "Soviet Union at the Olympics") did not participate until the [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") in Helsinki. Instead, starting in 1928, the Soviets organised an international sports event called [Spartakiads](/wiki/Spartakiad "Spartakiad"). During the interwar period of the 1920s and 1930s, communist and socialist organisations in several countries, including the United States, attempted to counter what they called the "bourgeois" Olympics with the [Workers Olympics](/wiki/Socialist_Workers%27_Sport_International "Socialist Workers' Sport International").[[212]](#cite_note-GSE-215)[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoche2000106-216) It was not until the [1956 Summer Games](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1956 Summer Olympics") that the Soviets emerged as a sporting superpower and, in doing so, took full advantage of the publicity that came with winning at the Olympics.[[214]](#cite_note-217) Soviet Union's success might be attributed to a heavy state's investment in sports to fulfill its political agenda on an international stage.[[215]](#cite_note-218)[[185]](#cite_note-cia.gov-188)

Individual athletes have also used the Olympic stage to promote their own political agenda. At the [1968 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics") in Mexico City, two American track and field athletes, [Tommie Smith](/wiki/Tommie_Smith "Tommie Smith") and [John Carlos](/wiki/John_Carlos "John Carlos"), who finished first and third in the 200 metres, performed the [Black Power salute](/wiki/1968_Olympics_Black_Power_salute "1968 Olympics Black Power salute") on the victory stand. The second-place finisher, [Peter Norman](/wiki/Peter_Norman "Peter Norman") of Australia, wore an [Olympic Project for Human Rights](/wiki/Olympic_Project_for_Human_Rights "Olympic Project for Human Rights") badge in support of Smith and Carlos. In response to the protest, IOC president [Avery Brundage](/wiki/Avery_Brundage "Avery Brundage") ordered Smith and Carlos suspended from the US team and banned from the Olympic Village. When the US Olympic Committee refused, Brundage threatened to ban the entire US track team. This threat led to the expulsion of the two athletes from the Games.[[216]](#cite_note-Black_Salute-219) In another notable incident in the gymnastics competition, while standing on the medal podium after the balance beam event final, in which [Natalia Kuchinskaya](/wiki/Natalia_Kuchinskaya "Natalia Kuchinskaya") of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") had controversially taken the gold, [Czechoslovakian](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") gymnast [Věra Čáslavská](/wiki/V%C4%9Bra_%C4%8C%C3%A1slavsk%C3%A1 "Věra Čáslavská") quietly turned her head down and away during the playing of the Soviet national anthem. The action was Čáslavská's silent protest against the recent [Soviet invasion of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact_invasion_of_Czechoslovakia "Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia"). Her protest was repeated when she accepted her medal for her floor exercise routine when the judges changed the preliminary scores of the Soviet [Larisa Petrik](/wiki/Larisa_Petrik "Larisa Petrik") to allow her to tie with Čáslavská for the gold. While Čáslavská's countrymen supported her actions and her outspoken opposition to communism (she had publicly signed and supported [Ludvik Vaculik](/wiki/Ludvik_Vaculik "Ludvik Vaculik")'s "[Two Thousand Words](/wiki/The_Two_Thousand_Words "The Two Thousand Words")" manifesto), the new regime responded by banning her from both sporting events and international travel for many years and made her an outcast from society until the fall of communism.

Currently, the government of Iran has taken steps to avoid any competition between its athletes and those from Israel. [Arash Miresmaeili](/wiki/Arash_Miresmaeili "Arash Miresmaeili"), an Iranian [judoka](/wiki/Judoka "Judoka"), did not compete in a match against an Israeli during the [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") in Athens. Although he was officially disqualified for being overweight, Miresmaeli was awarded US$125,000 in prize money by the Iranian government, an amount paid to all Iranian gold medal winners. He was officially cleared of intentionally avoiding the bout, but his receipt of the prize money raised suspicion.[[217]](#cite_note-220)

After the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the IOC Executive Board recommended "no participation of Russian and Belarusian athletes and officials, urges International Sports Federations and organisers of sports events worldwide to do everything in their power to ensure that no athlete or sports official from Russia or Belarus be allowed to take part under the name of Russia or Belarus".[[218]](#cite_note-221) In 2023, the IOC announced that Russian and Belarusian athletes could participate in the Olympics under certain conditions: they must not represent their country or any associated organisation, and those actively supporting the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") would be barred from competing. This decision aimed to allow athletes to compete and enhance their stature. While 32 athletes accepted the invitation, 28 qualified athletes declined. Competing under a neutral flag and uniform, these athletes had a neutral song played if they won any medals, instead of their national anthems. Additionally, the audience was prohibited from waving Russian and Belarusian flags.[[219]](#cite_note-222)

### Use of performance-enhancing drugs

Main article: [Doping at the Olympic Games](/wiki/Doping_at_the_Olympic_Games "Doping at the Olympic Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Marathon_Hicks1904.jpg/250px-Marathon_Hicks1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marathon_Hicks1904.jpg)

[Thomas Hicks](/wiki/Thomas_Hicks_(athlete) "Thomas Hicks (athlete)") running the [marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon") at the [1904 Olympics](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis")

In the early 20th century, many Olympic athletes began using drugs to improve their athletic abilities. For example, in 1904, [Thomas Hicks](/wiki/Thomas_Hicks_(athlete) "Thomas Hicks (athlete)"), a gold medallist in the marathon, was given [strychnine](/wiki/Strychnine "Strychnine") by his coach (at the time, taking different substances was allowed, as there was no data regarding the effect of these substances on a body of an athlete).[[220]](#cite_note-223) The only Olympic death linked to performance enhancing occurred at the [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). A Danish cyclist, [Knud Enemark Jensen](/wiki/Knud_Enemark_Jensen "Knud Enemark Jensen"), fell from his bicycle and later died. A coroner's inquiry found that he was under the influence of [amphetamines](/wiki/Amphetamines "Amphetamines").[[221]](#cite_note-wadahistory-224) By the mid-1960s, sports federations started to ban the use of performance-enhancing drugs; in 1967, the IOC followed suit.[[222]](#cite_note-225)

According to British journalist [Andrew Jennings](/wiki/Andrew_Jennings "Andrew Jennings"), a [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB") colonel stated that the agency's officers had posed as anti-doping authorities from the [International Olympic Committed](/w/index.php?title=International_Olympic_Committed&action=edit&redlink=1 "International Olympic Committed (page does not exist)") to undermine [doping tests](/wiki/Doping_test "Doping test") and that Soviet athletes were "rescued with [these] tremendous efforts".[[223]](#cite_note-DG2011-226) On the topic of the [1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics"), a 1989 Australian study said "There is hardly a medal winner at the Moscow Games, certainly not a gold medal winner, who is not on one sort of drug or another: usually several kinds. The Moscow Games might as well have been called the Chemists' Games."[[223]](#cite_note-DG2011-226)

In 2016, documents obtained revealed the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")'s plans for a statewide doping system in [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") in preparation for the [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Dated prior to the country's decision to boycott the Games, the document detailed the existing steroids operations of the program, along with suggestions for further enhancements.[[224]](#cite_note-NYT160813-227) The communication, directed to the Soviet Union's head of track and field, was prepared by Sergei Portugalov of the Institute for Physical Culture. Portugalov was also one of the main figures involved in the implementation of the Russian doping programme prior to the 2016 Summer Olympics.[[224]](#cite_note-NYT160813-227)

The first Olympic athlete to test positive for the use of performance-enhancing drugs was [Hans-Gunnar Liljenwall](/wiki/Hans-Gunnar_Liljenwall "Hans-Gunnar Liljenwall"), a Swedish [pentathlete](/wiki/Modern_pentathlon "Modern pentathlon") at the [1968 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics"), who lost his bronze medal for alcohol use.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorterfield200815-228) One of the most publicised doping-related disqualifications occurred after the [1988 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics") where Canadian sprinter [Ben Johnson](/wiki/Ben_Johnson_(Canadian_sprinter) "Ben Johnson (Canadian sprinter)") (who won the [100-metre dash](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1988_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_100_metres "Athletics at the 1988 Summer Olympics – Men's 100 metres")) tested positive for [stanozolol](/wiki/Stanozolol "Stanozolol").[[226]](#cite_note-229)

In 1999, the IOC formed the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (WADA) in an effort to systematise the research and detection of performance-enhancing drugs. There was a sharp increase in positive drug tests at the [2000 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics") and [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics") due to improved testing conditions. Several medallists in weightlifting and cross-country skiing from post-Soviet states were disqualified because of doping offences. The IOC-established drug testing regimen (now known as the Olympic Standard) has set the worldwide benchmark that other sporting federations attempt to emulate.[[227]](#cite_note-230) During the Beijing games, 3,667 athletes were tested by the IOC under the auspices of the World Anti-Doping Agency. Both urine and blood tests were used to detect banned substances.[[221]](#cite_note-wadahistory-224)[[228]](#cite_note-231) In London over 6,000 Olympic and Paralympic athletes were tested. Prior to the Games, 107 athletes tested positive for banned substances and were not allowed to compete.[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[230]](#cite_note-233)[[231]](#cite_note-234)

In 2024, rumors about China's doping spread as many of the athletes, especially in swimming, were accused of doping before Tokyo 2020. 23 Chinese swimmers were tested positive for prohibited substances, with both the World Anti-Doping Agency and World Aquatics accepting Chinese claims that positive tests were caused by tainted food.[[232]](#cite_note-235) After the FBI and Justice Department started investigating, the IOC threatened to revoke the 2034 Olympics in Salt Lake City, unless they dropped the investigation.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

#### Russian doping scandal

Further information: [Doping in Russia](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia"), [McLaren Report](/wiki/McLaren_Report "McLaren Report"), [Russia at the 2012 Summer Olympics § Russian doping scandal](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics#Russian_doping_scandal "Russia at the 2012 Summer Olympics"), [Russia at the 2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2014 Winter Olympics"), [Russia at the 2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Russia at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Olympic Athletes from Russia at the 2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Athletes_from_Russia_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Olympic Athletes from Russia at the 2018 Winter Olympics"), and [Oswald Commission](/wiki/Oswald_Commission "Oswald Commission")

[Doping](/wiki/Doping_in_sport "Doping in sport") in [Russian sports](/wiki/Russian_sports "Russian sports") has a systemic nature. [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_Olympics "Russia at the Olympics") has had 44 [Olympic medals stripped](/wiki/List_of_stripped_Olympic_medals "List of stripped Olympic medals") for doping violations—the most of any country, and more than a quarter of the global total. From 2011 to 2015, more than a thousand Russian competitors in various sports, including summer, winter, and Paralympic sports, benefited from a [cover-up](/wiki/Cover-up "Cover-up").[[234]](#cite_note-EDP-237)[[235]](#cite_note-McLarenII-238)[[236]](#cite_note-NYT161209-239)[[237]](#cite_note-Guardian161209-240) Russia was partially banned from the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") and was banned from the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") (while still being allowed to participate as the "[Olympic Athletes from Russia](/wiki/Olympic_Athletes_from_Russia "Olympic Athletes from Russia")") due to the state-sponsored doping programme.[[238]](#cite_note-241)[[239]](#cite_note-242)

In December 2019, Russia was banned for four years from all major sporting events for systematic doping and lying to WADA.[[240]](#cite_note-243) The ban was issued by WADA on 9 December 2019, and the Russian anti-doping agency RUSADA had 21 days to make an appeal to the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport") (CAS). The ban meant that Russian athletes would only be allowed to compete under the Olympic flag after passing anti-doping tests.[[241]](#cite_note-244) Russia appealed the decision to the CAS.[[242]](#cite_note-245) On review of Russia's appeal of its case from WADA, CAS ruled on 17 December 2020 to reduce the penalty that WADA had placed. Instead of banning Russia from sporting events, the ruling allowed Russia to participate at the Olympics and other international events, but for a period of two years the team cannot use the Russian name, flag, or anthem and must present themselves as "Neutral Athlete" or "Neutral Team". The ruling does allow for team uniforms to display "Russia" on the uniform as well as the use of the Russian flag colours within the uniform's design, although the name should be up to equal predominance as the "Neutral Athlete/Team" designation.[[243]](#cite_note-246)

In February 2022, during the Beijing Olympics, the international news media reported that the issue of doping was again raised over a positive test for [trimetazidine](/wiki/Trimetazidine "Trimetazidine") by the ROC's [Kamila Valieva](/wiki/Kamila_Valieva "Kamila Valieva"),[[244]](#cite_note-247)[[245]](#cite_note-248) which was officially confirmed on 11 February.[[246]](#cite_note-249) Valieva's sample in question was taken by the [Russian Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/Russian_Anti-Doping_Agency "Russian Anti-Doping Agency") (RUSADA) at the [2022 Russian Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/2022_Russian_Figure_Skating_Championships "2022 Russian Figure Skating Championships") on 25 December, but the sample was not analyzed at the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (WADA) laboratory where it was sent for testing until 8 February, one day after the team event concluded.[[247]](#cite_note-250) CAS was expected to hear the case on 13 February with a decision scheduled for announcement the next day, ahead of her scheduled appearance in the women's singles event beginning on 15 February.[[248]](#cite_note-251)[[249]](#cite_note-252) Due to Valieva being a minor at the time, as well as being classified as a "protected person" under WADA guidelines, RUSADA and the IOC announced on 12 February that they would broaden the scope of their respective investigations to include members of her entourage (e.g. coaches, team doctors, etc.).[[250]](#cite_note-253) By the end of the Beijing Olympics, a total five athletes were reported for doping violations.[[251]](#cite_note-254) A decision by RUSADA, issued in mid-October and endorsed by WADA, stated that the details of the Valieva hearing and its scheduled dates would be placed under international guidelines for the protection of minors (Valieva was 15 years old when the positive test results were disclosed) and would not be disclosed publicly.[[252]](#cite_note-Evan_Bates-255) Although Russia as a country is currently banned from participating in international skating events due to the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), Valieva has continued to compete within Russian borders without being hindered by RUSADA as recently as the Russian Grand Prix held in October 2022.[[253]](#cite_note-256) On 28 September 2023, CAS "ordered the production of further documentation" and announced that her hearing had been adjourned until 9‍–‍10 November. It appeared that one of the parties in the case requested a file that had not previously been a part of the proceedings.[[254]](#cite_note-USA_TODAY_2023_z927-257) On 10 November, CAS announced that a final decision was expected by the end of January 2024.[[255]](#cite_note-Anon._r079-258)[[256]](#cite_note-USA_TODAY_2023_q217-259) On 29 January 2024, the CAS disqualified Valieva for four years retroactive to 25 December 2021 for an anti-doping rule violation.[[257]](#cite_note-260)[[258]](#cite_note-261)

### Sex discrimination

Main article: [Participation of women in the Olympics](/wiki/Participation_of_women_in_the_Olympics "Participation of women in the Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Charlotte_Cooper.jpg/250px-Charlotte_Cooper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charlotte_Cooper.jpg)

[Charlotte Cooper](/wiki/Charlotte_Cooper_(tennis) "Charlotte Cooper (tennis)") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics"), the first female Olympic champion, in the [1900 Games](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics")

Women were first allowed to compete at the [1900 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics") in Paris, but at the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics") 35 countries were still only fielding all-male delegations.[[259]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-262) This number dropped rapidly over the following years. In 2000, [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_at_the_Olympics "Bahrain at the Olympics") sent two women competitors for the first time: [Fatema Hameed Gerashi](/wiki/Fatema_Hameed_Gerashi "Fatema Hameed Gerashi") and [Mariam Mohamed Hadi Al Hilli](/wiki/Mariam_Mohamed_Hadi_Al_Hilli "Mariam Mohamed Hadi Al Hilli").[[260]](#cite_note-263) In 2004, [Robina Muqimyar](/wiki/Robina_Muqimyar "Robina Muqimyar") and [Fariba Rezayee](/wiki/Fariba_Rezayee "Fariba Rezayee") became the first women to compete for [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_at_the_Olympics "Afghanistan at the Olympics").[[261]](#cite_note-264) In 2008, the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_at_the_Olympics "United Arab Emirates at the Olympics") sent female athletes for the first time; [Maitha Al Maktoum](/wiki/Maitha_Al_Maktoum "Maitha Al Maktoum") competed in taekwondo, and [Latifa Al Maktoum](/wiki/Latifa_bint_Ahmed_Al_Maktoum "Latifa bint Ahmed Al Maktoum") in equestrian. Both athletes were from [Dubai's ruling family](/wiki/House_of_Maktoum "House of Maktoum").[[262]](#cite_note-265)

By 2010, only three countries had never sent female athletes to the Games: [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Olympics "Brunei at the Olympics"), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_at_the_Olympics "Saudi Arabia at the Olympics"), and [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_at_the_Olympics "Qatar at the Olympics"). Brunei had taken part in only three celebrations of the Games, sending a single athlete on each occasion, but Saudi Arabia and Qatar had been competing regularly with all-male teams. In 2010, the International Olympic Committee announced it would "press" these countries to enable and facilitate the participation of women for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") in London. [Anita DeFrantz](/wiki/Anita_DeFrantz "Anita DeFrantz"), chair of the IOC's Women and Sports Commission, suggested that countries be barred if they prevented women from competing. Shortly thereafter, the [Qatar Olympic Committee](/wiki/Qatar_Olympic_Committee "Qatar Olympic Committee") announced that it "hoped to send up to four female athletes in [shooting](/wiki/Shooting_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Shooting at the 2012 Summer Olympics") and [fencing](/wiki/Fencing_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Fencing at the 2012 Summer Olympics")" to the 2012 Summer Games.[[263]](#cite_note-266)

In 2008, [Ali al-Ahmed](/wiki/Ali_al-Ahmed "Ali al-Ahmed"), director of the [Institute for Gulf Affairs](/wiki/Institute_for_Gulf_Affairs "Institute for Gulf Affairs"), likewise called for Saudi Arabia to be barred from the Games, describing its ban on women athletes as a violation of the International Olympic Committee charter. He noted: "For the last 15 years, many international nongovernmental organisations worldwide have been trying to lobby the IOC for better enforcement of its own laws banning gender discrimination.  While their efforts did result in increasing numbers of women Olympians, the IOC has been reluctant to take a strong position and threaten the discriminating countries with suspension or expulsion."[[259]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-262) In July 2010, *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* reported: "Pressure is growing on the International Olympic Committee to kick out Saudi Arabia, who are likely to be the only major nation not to include women in their Olympic team for 2012. ... Should Saudi Arabia ... send a male-only team to London, we understand they will face protests from equal rights and women's groups which threaten to disrupt the Games".[[264]](#cite_note-independent.co.uk-267)

At the 2012 Summer Olympics, every participating nation included female athletes for the first time in Olympic history.[[265]](#cite_note-268) Saudi Arabia included two female athletes in its delegation; Qatar, four; and Brunei, one ([Maziah Mahusin](/wiki/Maziah_Mahusin "Maziah Mahusin"), in the 400 m hurdles). Qatar made one of its first female Olympians, [Bahiya al-Hamad](/wiki/Bahiya_al-Hamad "Bahiya al-Hamad") (shooting), its flagbearer at the 2012 Games,[[266]](#cite_note-269) and runner [Maryam Yusuf Jamal](/wiki/Maryam_Yusuf_Jamal "Maryam Yusuf Jamal") of [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") became the first Persian Gulf female athlete to win a medal when she won a bronze for her showing in the [1500 m race](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_1500_metres "Athletics at the 2012 Summer Olympics – Women's 1500 metres").[[267]](#cite_note-270)

The only sports on the Olympic programme that features men and women individually competing against one another are the equestrian disciplines, as there is no "Women's Eventing", or "Men's Dressage". As of 2008, there were still more medal events for men than women. With the addition of women's boxing to the programme in the 2012 Summer Olympics, however, women athletes were able to compete in all the sports open to men.[[268]](#cite_note-271) In the winter Olympics, women are still unable to compete in the [Nordic combined](/wiki/Nordic_combined "Nordic combined").[[269]](#cite_note-272) After men were included in artistic swimming at the Paris 2024 games,[[270]](#cite_note-273) the only remaining Olympic event in which male athletes may not compete is [rhythmic gymnastics](/wiki/Rhythmic_gymnastics "Rhythmic gymnastics").[[271]](#cite_note-274) Despite being eligible to qualify for the 2024 Paris games, no men were included on any artistic swimming team.[[272]](#cite_note-275)

A recent addition to the Games has been the inclusion of mixed events, whereby men and women of the same nation compete together against other teams. Beginning in 2018, the sport of [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling") introduced a mixed event where teams of one man and one women competed in their own tournament for a medal. As of the 2024 Paris games, there are now 13 mixed medal events across 11 disciplines at the summer games.[[273]](#cite_note-276)

### War and terrorism

The [world wars](/wiki/World_war "World war") caused three Olympiads to pass without a celebration of the Games: the [1916 Games](/wiki/1916_Summer_Olympics "1916 Summer Olympics") were cancelled because of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and the summer and winter games of 1940 and 1944 were cancelled because of World War II.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxii-48) The [Russo-Georgian War](/wiki/Russo-Georgian_War "Russo-Georgian War") between Georgia and Russia erupted on the opening day of the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics") in Beijing. Both American President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and Russian Prime Minister [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") were attending the Olympics at that time and spoke together about the conflict at a luncheon hosted by the Chinese president [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao").[[274]](#cite_note-MSNBC_2009-01-30-277)[[275]](#cite_note-278)

Terrorism most directly affected the Olympic Games in 1972. When the [Summer Games](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") were held in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), Germany, eleven members of the [Israeli Olympic team](/wiki/Israel_at_the_1972_Summer_Olympics "Israel at the 1972 Summer Olympics") were taken hostage by the [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") terrorist group [Black September](/wiki/Black_September_Organization "Black September Organization") in what is now known as the [Munich massacre](/wiki/Munich_massacre "Munich massacre"). The terrorists killed two of the athletes soon after taking them hostage and killed the other nine during a failed liberation attempt. A German police officer and five of the terrorists also died.[[276]](#cite_note-279) Following the selection of [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), Spain, to host the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"), the separatist [ETA](/wiki/ETA_(separatist_group) "ETA (separatist group)") terrorist organisation launched attacks in the region, including the [1991 bombing](/wiki/Vic_bombing "Vic bombing") in the Catalonian city of [Vic](/wiki/Vic,_Catalonia "Vic, Catalonia") that killed ten people.[[277]](#cite_note-280)[[278]](#cite_note-281)

Terrorism affected two Olympic Games held in the United States. During the [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics") in Atlanta, [a bomb was detonated at the Centennial Olympic Park](/wiki/Centennial_Olympic_Park_bombing "Centennial Olympic Park bombing"), killing two people and injuring 111 others. The bomb was set by [Eric Rudolph](/wiki/Eric_Rudolph "Eric Rudolph"), who is serving a life sentence for the bombing.[[279]](#cite_note-282) The [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics") in Salt Lake City took place just five months after the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), which meant a higher level of security than ever before provided for an Olympic Games. The opening ceremonies of the Games featured symbols relating to 9/11, including the flag that flew at [Ground Zero](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site "World Trade Center site") and honour guards of [NYPD](/wiki/NYPD "NYPD") and [FDNY](/wiki/FDNY "FDNY") members.[[280]](#cite_note-283)

## Citizenship

### IOC rules for citizenship

The Olympic Charter requires that an athlete be a [legal national](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality") of the country for which they compete. Dual nationals may compete for either country, as long as three years have passed since the competitor competed for the former country. However, if the NOCs and IF involved agree, then the IOC Executive Board may reduce or cancel this period.[[281]](#cite_note-Olympic_Charter_2011-284) This waiting period exists only for athletes who previously competed for one nation and want to compete for another. If an athlete gains a new or second nationality, then they do not need to wait any designated amount of time before participating for the new or second nation. The IOC is only concerned with issues of citizenship and nationality after individual nations have granted citizenship to athletes.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShachar20112114–2116-285)

In October 2023, the IOC suspended the [Russian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee "Russian Olympic Committee") for incorporating the [four annexed Ukrainian regions](/wiki/Russian_annexation_of_Donetsk,_Kherson,_Luhansk_and_Zaporizhzhia_oblasts "Russian annexation of Donetsk, Kherson, Luhansk and Zaporizhzhia oblasts") into its charter, and the suspension was upheld in February 2024 by [CAS](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport").[[283]](#cite_note-286) Without any NOC from Russia to approve a change of citizenship in under three years from Russia to another country, the IOC Executive Board approved changes of nationality in November 2023
and March 2024 for a total of five athletes from Russia to another country, including [Georgi Tiblov](/wiki/Georgii_Tibilov_(wrestler,_born_2000) "Georgii Tibilov (wrestler, born 2000)") and [Aleksandr Komarov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Komarov_(wrestler) "Aleksandr Komarov (wrestler)") in wrestling (from Russia to Serbia), [Mikhail Yakovlev](/wiki/Mikhail_Iakovlev "Mikhail Iakovlev") in cycling (from Russia to Israel), [Valeriia Liubimova](/w/index.php?title=Valeriia_Liubimova&action=edit&redlink=1 "Valeriia Liubimova (page does not exist)") in cycling, and [Anastasiia Kirpichnikova](/wiki/Anastasiia_Kirpichnikova "Anastasiia Kirpichnikova") in swimming (both from Russia to France).[[284]](#cite_note-287)[[285]](#cite_note-288)[[286]](#cite_note-289)[[287]](#cite_note-290)

### Reasons for changing citizenship

Occasionally, an athlete will become a citizen of a different country to enable them to compete in the Olympics. This is often because they are attracted to sponsorship deals or training facilities in the other country, or the athlete might be unable to qualify from within their country of birth. In preparation for the 2014 Winter Olympics in Sochi, the Russian Olympic Committee naturalised a South Korean-born short-track speed-skater, [Ahn Hyun-soo](/wiki/Ahn_Hyun-soo "Ahn Hyun-soo"), and an American-born snowboarder, [Vic Wild](/wiki/Vic_Wild "Vic Wild"). The two athletes won five gold medals and one bronze medal between them at the 2014 Games.[[288]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-291)

## Champions and medallists

Further information: [Lists of Olympic medallists](/wiki/Lists_of_Olympic_medallists "Lists of Olympic medallists") and [List of multiple Olympic gold medallists](/wiki/List_of_multiple_Olympic_gold_medallists "List of multiple Olympic gold medallists")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Paavo_Nurmi_%28Paris_1924%29.jpg/250px-Paavo_Nurmi_%28Paris_1924%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paavo_Nurmi_(Paris_1924).jpg)

[Paavo Nurmi](/wiki/Paavo_Nurmi "Paavo Nurmi"), legendary [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland") [middle-distance](/wiki/Middle-distance_running "Middle-distance running") and [long-distance runner](/wiki/Long-distance_runner "Long-distance runner"), nicknamed the "[Flying Finn](/wiki/Flying_Finn "Flying Finn")", set 22 [world records](/wiki/List_of_world_records_in_athletics "List of world records in athletics") at distances between [1500 m](/wiki/1500_metres "1500 metres") and 20 km, winning nine [gold](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal") and three [silver](/wiki/Silver_medal "Silver medal") medals in the 1920 through 1928 Olympic Games.

Medals are awarded to the athletes or teams who place first, second, and third in each event. The winners receive gold medals, which were solid gold until 1912, later made of gilded silver, and now gold-plated silver. Every gold medal must contain at least six grams of pure gold.[[289]](#cite_note-BeijingOlymp-1-292) The runners-up are awarded silver medals and the third-place athletes receive bronze medals. In events contested by a [single-elimination tournament](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament") (most notably boxing), third place might not be determined and the losers of both semi-finals each receive a bronze medal.

At the [1896 Olympics](/wiki/1896_Summer_Olympics "1896 Summer Olympics"), only the winner and runner-up of each event received medals—silver for first and bronze for second, with no gold medals awarded. The current three-medal format was introduced at the [1904 Olympics](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics").[[290]](#cite_note-293) From 1948 onward, athletes placing fourth, fifth, and sixth have received certificates, which came to be known officially as [Olympic diplomas](/wiki/Olympic_diploma "Olympic diploma"); from 1984, these have also been awarded to the seventh- and eighth-place finishers. At the [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") in Athens, the gold, silver, and bronze medal winners were also presented with [olive wreaths](/wiki/Olive_wreath "Olive wreath").[[291]](#cite_note-294) The [IOC](/wiki/IOC "IOC") does not keep statistics of medals won on a national level (except for team sports), but the NOCs and the media record medal statistics and use them as a measure of each nation's success.[[292]](#cite_note-295)

## Nations

### Participants

Main articles: [List of participating nations at the Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/List_of_participating_nations_at_the_Summer_Olympic_Games "List of participating nations at the Summer Olympic Games") and [List of participating nations at the Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/List_of_participating_nations_at_the_Winter_Olympic_Games "List of participating nations at the Winter Olympic Games")

As of the [2020 Summer Games](/wiki/2020_Summer_Games "2020 Summer Games") in Tokyo, all of the current 207 NOCs (and 19 obsolete NOCs) have participated in at least one edition of the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics"). Competitors from five nations—[Australia](/wiki/Australia_at_the_Olympics "Australia at the Olympics"), [France](/wiki/France_at_the_Olympics "France at the Olympics"),[[d]](#cite_note-298) [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics"),[[e]](#cite_note-300) [Greece](/wiki/Greece_at_the_Olympics "Greece at the Olympics"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_Olympics "Switzerland at the Olympics")[[f]](#cite_note-303)—have competed in all 28 Summer Olympics. Athletes competing under the Olympic flag, Mixed Teams and the [Refugee Team](/wiki/Refugee_Olympic_Team_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Refugee Olympic Team at the 2016 Summer Olympics") have competed at six Summer Olympics Games.

A total of 119 NOCs (110 of the current 207 NOCs, and nine obsolete NOCs) have participated in at least one edition of the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics"). Competitors from 12 nations—[Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_Olympics "Austria at the Olympics"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_Olympics "Canada at the Olympics"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_Olympics "Finland at the Olympics"), France, Great Britain, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_at_the_Olympics "Hungary at the Olympics"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_Olympics "Italy at the Olympics"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_Olympics "Norway at the Olympics"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland_at_the_Olympics "Poland at the Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_Olympics "Sweden at the Olympics"), Switzerland, and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Olympics "United States at the Olympics")—have participated in all 23 Winter Games to date.

### Host nations and cities

Main article: [List of Olympic Games host cities](/wiki/List_of_Olympic_Games_host_cities "List of Olympic Games host cities")

See also: [List of Olympic medals by host nation](/wiki/List_of_Olympic_medals_by_host_nation "List of Olympic medals by host nation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Summer_Olympics.svg/250px-Summer_Olympics.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Summer_Olympics.svg)

Map of [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") locations with countries that have hosted one Summer Olympics shaded in green and countries that have hosted two or more shaded in blue

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Winter_olympics_all_cities.svg/250px-Winter_olympics_all_cities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Winter_olympics_all_cities.svg)

Map of [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") locations with countries that have hosted one Winter Olympics shaded in green and countries that have hosted two or more shaded in blue

The host city for an Olympic Games had historically been chosen seven to eight years ahead of their celebration. Beginning with the 2024 and 2028 Olympics selection process in 2017, the IOC has proceeded to announce the winning bid with a longer lead-in time in order to provide time for the winning cities/regions to prepare.[[298]](#cite_note-bidpro-304)[[299]](#cite_note-305) The process of selection is carried out in two phases that span a two-year period. The prospective host city applies to its country's National Olympic Committee; if more than one city from the same country submits a proposal to its NOC, the national committee typically holds an internal selection, since only one city per NOC can be presented to the International Olympic Committee for consideration. Once the deadline for submission of proposals by the NOCs is reached, the first phase (Application) begins with the applicant cities asked to complete a questionnaire regarding several key criteria related to the organisation of the Olympic Games.[[300]](#cite_note-hostcity-306) In this form, the applicants must give assurances that they will comply with the Olympic Charter and with any other regulations established by the IOC Executive Committee.[[298]](#cite_note-bidpro-304) The evaluation of the filled questionnaires by a specialised group provides the IOC with an overview of each applicant's project and their potential to host the Games. On the basis of this technical evaluation, the IOC Executive Board selects the applicants that will proceed to the candidature stage.[[300]](#cite_note-hostcity-306)

Once the candidate cities are selected, they must submit to the IOC a bigger and more detailed presentation of their project as part of a candidature file. Each city is thoroughly analysed by an evaluation commission. This commission will also visit the candidate cities, interviewing local officials and inspecting prospective venue sites, before submitting a report on its findings one month prior to the IOC's final decision. During the interview process the candidate city must also guarantee that it will be able to fund the Games.[[298]](#cite_note-bidpro-304) After the work of the evaluation commission, a list of candidates is presented to the General Session of the IOC, which must assemble in a country that does not have a candidate city in the running. The IOC members gathered in the Session have the final vote on the host city. Once elected, the host city bid committee (together with the NOC of the respective country) signs a Host City Contract with the IOC, officially becoming an Olympic host nation and host city.[[298]](#cite_note-bidpro-304)

By 2032, the Olympic Games will have been hosted by 47 cities in 23 countries. As of 2021, since the 1988 Summer Olympics in Seoul, South Korea, the Olympics have been held in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") or [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") four times, a sharp increase compared to the previous 92 years of modern Olympic history. The 2016 Games in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") were the first Olympics for a South American country. No bids from countries in Africa have succeeded, although Egypt (thrice) and South Africa (in 2004) have tried.[[301]](#cite_note-307)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** English and French are the official languages of the Olympic Movement.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** However, Theodosius' decree contains no specific reference to [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrowther200754-28)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-54)** The [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") were in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), Canada, and the [1988 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Paralympics "1988 Winter Paralympics") were in [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck"), Austria.[[51]](#cite_note-53)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-298)** The IOC lists a French immigrant to the United States [Albert Corey](/wiki/Albert_Corey "Albert Corey") as a United States competitor for his marathon silver medal, but he is listed (together with four undisputed Americans) as part of a mixed team for the team race silver medal.[[293]](#cite_note-OR1904-296)[[294]](#cite_note-IOCdatabase-297)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-300)** All three of [Great Britain's athletes in 1904](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_1904_Summer_Olympics "Great Britain at the 1904 Summer Olympics") were from Ireland, which at the time was part of the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). Despite the team being called "Great Britain" or "Team GB", inhabitants of Northern Ireland (and formerly the whole of Ireland) are/were eligible to join.[[295]](#cite_note-299)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-303)** Switzerland participated in the [equestrian events of the 1956 Games](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the 1956 Summer Olympics") held in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") in June,[[296]](#cite_note-EQ1956-301) but did not attend the Games in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") later that year.[[297]](#cite_note-OR1956-302)
